title: Edit Distance
problem_statement: Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations required to convert word1 to word2. You have the following three operations permitted on a word: Insert a character, Delete a character, Replace a character.

examples.explanation: horse -> rorse (replace 'h' with 'r'), rorse -> rose (delete 'r'), rose -> ros (delete 'e').
Use the two pointers method to see the differences.

constraints: word1 and word2 consist of lowercase English letters.
intuition: This is a classic dynamic programming problem where we define dp[i][j] as the minimum edits to make word1[0..i-1] equal to word2[0..j-1].

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: Recurse on the strings, at each step choosing to match, insert, delete, or replace. Base: if one string is empty, edits = length of other.
approaches.why_not: Exponential time due to trying all edit sequences. Overlapping subproblems are recomputed.
interview_commentary: This is the naive recursive approach. At each position, if chars match, move both pointers. If not, try the three operations: replace (move both), delete (move i), insert (move j).
The recursion tree is huge because for each mismatch, we branch into 3 choices.
This will work for very small strings but TLE for lengths > 10.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion (Top-Down DP)
approaches.description: Same recursion, but memoize on (i, j) to store minimum edits for prefixes.
approaches.why_not: Bottom-up is iterative and avoids recursion stack issues.
interview_commentary: To fix the exponential time, I'll memoize on the pair (i, j) — the lengths of the remaining strings.
This ensures each subproblem is solved once, bringing time to O(m*n).
Space is O(m*n) for the memo table.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming
approaches.description: Fill a 2D dp table. dp[i][j] = min edits for word1[0..i-1] to word2[0..j-1]. Base: dp[i][0] = i, dp[0][j] = j. For each cell, if chars match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1], else 1 + min of the three operations.
approaches.why_not: This is the standard optimal solution. Space can be optimized to O(min(m,n)) with two arrays.
interview_commentary: Bottom-up: initialize the first row and column for empty strings.
Then for each i,j, if chars match, take dp[i-1][j-1]. Else, take 1 + min of replace (dp[i-1][j-1]), delete (dp[i-1][j]), insert (dp[i][j-1]).
This fills the table in order, using previously computed values.

interview_qa.q: What is the significance of dp[i][j]?
interview_qa.a: It represents the minimum edits to transform the first i characters of word1 into the first j characters of word2.

interview_qa.q: Why are there three choices when characters don't match?
interview_qa.a: Replace: change the char and move both pointers. Delete: remove from word1, move i. Insert: add to word1, move j.
Yes, since dp[i][j] only depends on the previous row, we can use two 1D arrays of size n+1, alternating them.

interview_qa.q: How does this relate to longest common subsequence?
interview_qa.a: Edit distance = len1 + len2 - 2*LCS, where LCS is the longest common subsequence.
